speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 3-- February 6th, 1995, interview with survivor Regina Gelb. And the interviewer's name is Chana [? Meier. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: One second.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no say on what we would be doing, whether we would be planning to run away or not. Because I was very young. My two sisters were truly in charge of me. In every respect, they-- they were father and mother. Even though Father was in the same camp, he was with the men so that we saw him every now and then. But basically, I was being mothered by my two sisters, and it was not for me to decide whether or not we will go as the others did. And as soon as the shooting started that night, where the heaps of bodies were found at the opening of that fence. One woman running either toward-- toward the-- the fence or away from the fence-- I can't tell-- was caught in the middle, and she was shot. She died in the worst agony of yelling and crying all night long. You see, the others that were shot at the-- at the fence were all dead, because obviously, the guards were right over them. I imagine that this was-- the fact that she was left to-- in the agony of this for hours, screaming and yelling, was probably done on purpose, because she could have just as well have been shot like the others were.

speaker: interviewer
text: I will never forget the agony. And she kept yelling that her legs were shot off. Whatever it was, I-- I cannot begin to tell you [SIGHS] what it-- what it felt like because at this point, we knew that something horrible was going to be happening to us. And we just didn't know how we were going to perish, you see? Whether we were going to perish like this woman, left to die in agony for hours rather than be finished off, or whether we gonna be in a heap like these people. And sure enough, next day, we were loaded on trains. Were you aware of this plan to escape beforehand?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Again, as I'm telling-- as I told you, I was always protected. Even though my parents-- my father was a-- in the camp, but he didn't mix. Even though this was not a family unit, my sisters were still my protectors. I was told what to do, and I did it because I loved them. And-- and we're very close to this day. I loved them dearly, and they loved me. And they said, now we do this, and now we do that. But it never really-- my input was zero, because all I had to do is to be alert of what was happening around. But decisions for escape or such, that-- really, I mean, I was really too young to-- and I was, don't forget, I was by then very skinny. I was a skinny child to begin with, you see. And then with the malnutrition and the starvation and all these. You had boils. Every now and then, I had boils. That's-- you know, this is all a result of malnutrition.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all these things that-- that combined to make you a wreck of a human being were visible in all of us. But because we were so tightly knit-- and we also had, they had, friends. We had friends, you know, who participated in this kind of support-- you see? The school friends, the friends of the family. Well, I shouldn't be saying that, but-- because human beings know how to-- how to come across when there's need, you know? When people see a little kid, and it's full of lice and starved to death, and she's all left alone, somebody will hug her, you see? Because the world is like this. On one end, you have this horror of the German beast with the dogs or the Ukrainian. On the other hand, you have this little old lady who will hug a little skinny kid filled with, covered with lice. So anyhow, my sisters were my protectors, and they were my guardians, and they were-- they were my surrogate parents. They were everything. And of course, the fact that every now and then we could see Father-- you know, wave to him or whatever-- was very helpful. We lived in barracks, of course, in that camp, [INAUDIBLE] But in the last camp where the breakout took place, that-- I don't even remember if there was a real camp or if they would just dumped us there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was transitional in a way of it probably took a day or two or three at the most. Because the trains that were on that siding-- because, as you know, a factory like this must have a siding near it for transporting the-- the the goods to the factory and back-- that siding was used for the trains onto which this last camp was emptied onto those trains. And this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Every one was emptied?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody that was not killed in the-- in the-- in the breakout the night before. Everybody who survived the horror of typhus, of starvation, of beating, of working to death in the factories. After all the-- the hundreds of people who died, those of us who were left in the last camp were loaded onto a train. And this was July 1944. We did not know why we were being sent out. We did not know where we were being sent out. But after the war and of course reading history, we found out. I learned that the Russian offensive had started in the East. And the Germans were now emptying all the camps, the so-called work camps-- which were slave labor camps-- that were in my area, where munition-- war-- the-- the-- The munitions factory that was full war production, the Germans did not feed you the piece of bread in the morning and that horrible soup in the evening because they loved you. They kept you as long as they could because their war effort was based on the labor-- slave labor of these people in my hometown and the towns around.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Russians started coming west, all these camps were emptied-- being emptied. And our camp went to Auschwitz. And you see, when we arrived there at the end of July 1945-- or maybe August-- beginning of August, something like this-- there was only one crematorium working.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were unloaded. We had no idea where we were. We were tattooed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, we stood in line. My sister Anna, the oldest one, got the first number. My sister [? Kris, ?] got the second number, and I got the third number. So that whenever there will be reference to numbers, we would find ourselves in the same unit, you see. We were sent into the so-called sauna, which was-- which was a shower. But they took away our clothes, and they just threw other clothes to us. And the-- the thing that happened there, they were not-- at one time, I understand in Auschwitz, they would shave women's heads. When our transport came, because now this was really the political situation and the-- the war was taking on another direction, they did not shave us. They just, you know, they sat you down. They took a pair of scissors, and they just chopped away some hair. You know how you would look like a-- you know, a horror, because this was long. This was short. This was bald. This-- whatever. Doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because in the old days, they would shave with a-- with one of these-- oh, what do you call these little machines? You know, these-- A razor? A shaver?

speaker: interviewee
text: Little machines that people shave people's heads. Anyhow, my oldest sister had a friend-- my oldest sister Anna was always very tall. And she had a friend who was just as tall. Irene is her name-- Irene Ross now. Well, anyhow, how-- however it was, my sister Anna was so incensed by the way we were treated. They spat on us. The women, I think, were Czechoslovak attendant women. You know. They called us names. They kicked us. They spit on us in that, move faster! And this-- and sister, my oldest sister, said, you have no right to treat me like that. Well, the-- her friend Irene said something similar. Says, you shouldn't be treating people that this-- something like that. Well, they were pulled back, sat down, and shaved to zero. Well, I must tell you. In all the horror of the situation-- the anxiety, the fear, not knowing where we are and what is happening. And-- and now where Father is, we didn't know either. Because Father was in the train with the men. We were with the women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyhow, in all that, Anna and Irene burst out laughing because they looked absolutely-- well, there is no description, you know? Grotesque is the closest, but it doesn't describe. These two, tall sticks of women with bald heads-- little-- little heads, you know, starved little faces, bald. Well, of course, this was what they call in Polish, 'hangman's humor,' you know. This is the type of laughter that really should have been hysterics.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, this was the women's section. So we were processed through tattooing, through head-- the head being shaved, whatever being thrown some kind of clothes, whatever it was. I mean, if you were a size 10, you got a size 52. And if you were a size 14, you got a size 6, whatever. You know, they just threw it at you, and if it didn't fit, too bad. Wooden shoes. And with-- at the time, when the train was unloaded in Auschwitz, we saw Father emerge from the train. It so happened that our wagon was adjacent to the men's. You know, the men's-- the men's wagons were a few, and they ended. And then the others started. Where we went in the first female wagon, and Father was in the last men's wagon. So when we were unloaded, on the platform in Auschwitz, we still saw Father there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But of course later on, he did not survive. Very soon, he became very ill because he was-- you see, after Mother-- after Mother was taken away, he changed terribly. He became totally gray and despondent, and he didn't have that spark in him any more and not a sense of humor. And I think he really lived because we were around. You know? And he-- he might as the situation was horrible for-- for all of us, I think that sustained him.

speaker: interviewee
text: But once we came to Auschwitz and we were totally separated-- he went with the men somewhere on the men's side. We didn't know. And we went somewhere else. We found out from people from my hometown who came with the same transport that he actually disintegrated in a very short time. So by October, I think, he-- he was-- he died. And of course-- of course I'm sure he went to crematory. And so we have never seen him again-- never after seeing him on the platform in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mother went-- Mother perished in Treblinka two years before that. And now Father perished, and now the three of us were left. Was there a selection when you arrived at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This is why, as I've told you before, they were very little kids in our camp in Starachowice, and these kids were let through. There were five- and six-year-olds. Because, you see, this was by then-- I understand now from reading and doing research on the subject-- that some of the crematories were gone and, you know, this was now, really, the heat was on the Germans. But of course, there was still plenty of suffering ahead of us, which we really didn't know. But-- and we didn't know why we were sent out of Starachowice. You see, we-- we were in the eye of the storm, but we never knew what was really happening as far as the war was concerned. We-- we-- we didn't know. I mean, we didn't have papers. We didn't-- nobody told us. I mean, we-- we had no idea that there was a Russian offensive from the East and there was going-- there was now a build up on the West-- in the West-- you know, that the Americans and the English were-- were trying to-- to bring an end to the war. But we know-- we knew nothing of that. We just knew that our suffering has now increased. And the rest of what was the bit of humanity that still remained with us in those-- in those camps in our hometown was now very much over. I mean, that was now-- it wasn't a surreal world. This was hell. I mean, now this was living hell, and-- and this was something that we couldn't even comprehend. I mean, we could comprehend being exhausted, and being hungry, and having lice, and working, and all. You know, and-- and-- and understanding the war effort, how they would squeeze the blood out of us for their war effort-- we could understand that, even I could. But this place, Auschwitz, it was something incomprehensible. I mean, we didn't really know the-- what the purpose of this place was, since we really never knew that this was a real killing camp-- that there were people coming in transport right into the-- into the crematories and into-- out the chimney. We had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this place didn't look like-- it had barbed wire that was electrified. You know. We were taken into a barracks-- again, a lot of us from my hometown, you see? We were still in the group. And we were placed in the barracks, and we were on a type of a quarantine for about a month in Auschwitz. And what does quarantine mean? That means that you were not 'privileged'-- quote, unquote-- 'privileged' to work outside of the camp. And there was nothing to do in the camp, so they had a pile of rocks on one end and a pile of rocks on the other end. They had one group of women carrying rocks from here to that pile, and then the other group from here to there. You see? This was to wear you out, to beat you down, to exhaust you so that they wouldn't even have to burn you anymore because you will do it two yourself, so to say. Did you work besides-- after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is only the first phase. The quarantine was about a month. And we arrived, I'd say, at the end of July, early August-- so probably all of August, we were in so-called quarantine. And then by September, we were sent to cut marshes. There were some escapees by then from Auschwitz-- not that we knew it. But I know now why we were assigned to cut the marshes. The reason was that apparently the Germans did not want marsh land around the camp for-- because these people who were running-- might want to run away-- would hide there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we were sent onto this job to cut marshes. We stood in water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was incredible. The food-- the-- the food that was given out in-- first of all, you know, in Auschwitz, you had to be out, counted-- you-- roll call, every morning. If they didn't like-- if somebody was missing, you could stand there for two hours. If they didn't like the way you lined up, you were beaten.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had-- they gave us what was called coffee, which was water-- which was nothing-- with a little piece of bread that was for the day. That was to last you the day. You had to line up, to leave through the gate that says, [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, 'Work makes you free.' Cut marsh in the marshes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We would walk. We would-- I don't really know how far, but it had to be a distance, a few miles. And there were these private homes along the way, and people were living lives in those homes. You know? There were geranium boxes in the window. There with pillows being aired. You know? Who these people were who lived there, I don't know. Could be the attendant, the Auschwitz attendant, the German attendants with their families or could be-- I don't know who. But those were homes as we knew homes to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were so far away from anything that could even be called human. And you know, that-- you think in pictures. You know. That picture of walking by the homes with the pillows in the windows and the geraniums and such, going to the marshes, standing there in water cutting the-- in the marshes, then coming back to be beaten and line up again. And if anybody didn't line up, you-- they could keep you there forever. And then going to sleep inside the barracks, nine people to a-- you know, they had bunks. One bunk had nine women. So we would sleep head to foot because nine the same way, the same direction, would not fit. So you had to-- to sleep one way, one this way. And the humiliations-- the daily humiliations of the people who were running the barracks. You know. I once remembered-- this was also a Czech woman. Her name was Aranka. I don't know-- or Slovak. I don't know. But they were-- she was one of these hefty, big women. And one day, I was passing by, she grabbed me by the head. She put her foot out on this chair-- she wore leather boots-- and told me to shine with my spit to shine her shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm standing there shivering, you know, because I was alone, and I don't know how she got hold of me. I think I was on the-- on the way outside. There was a latrine in the back. I think I was on the way to the latrine. Anyhow, I was afraid if I don't do it, she herself could kill me. I mean, with one blow. You know, one blow on the head from her, it would have done it too. And those-- and even though there was-- we were by now so totally below a level of any humanity, it really-- it really bothered me, you know, this thing. I mean, why should I be bothered about the fact that she told me to clean her boots by spitting on it then shining when I was going through all of this-- the deprivation, and the hunger, and the lice, and the hard work, and not knowing about my father, and being completely at the mercy of God knows what? Why to-- why be humiliated by that? I never forgot it. It was the utmost-- I still don't know-- you know, I can't put it in-- in a perspective to analyze. Why would that have bothered me so much?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess it was because it came from an individual, a single individual woman. It wasn't the German with the dog who hit me with his-- I was once hit on the-- during quarantine. He had a big gold ring, and he hit me. And I remember how blood splattered. I mean this, I didn't-- this was par for the course. To be hit in the face and to bleed profusely is one thing-- by a dog-- by a gentleman with a dog. But to have Aranka pick me and-- and stand over me, threatening me to clean her shoes, that struck me as something totally-- And it's ridiculous, really. I mean, if you considered whole picture, this was really nothing. And this isn't even worth remembering. But I do remember things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you said in the other camps, you experienced a lot altruism, and those people from your community really took care of you. Did you feel that way in the barracks in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, among each other. Among the prisoners, yes. We were very attached to the group because, as I told you, these were still people from our hometown, you see. And we worked in those marshes. I don't know how long, but then a call came for 30 women to be sent to another job. And we were picked. As I told you, we had the three numbers in consecutive order.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the numbers were called, and we were sent to a job. Well, that was a godsend. That saved our lives, really, because it was an indoor job. It was within the Auschwitz camp, but it was a-- it was a unit of-- of-- that was self-containing unit, separated from the population-- Auschwitz speculation-- from the camp. It used to be called-- it was called a [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And it dealt with clothing of political prisoners who-- you know, I'm not talking about Jews now.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, Auschwitz was also a camp-- aside from being a killing camp for the Jews, it was also a camp, a concentration camp, for prisoners of other nationalities and other faiths. And when these prisoners would be sent into Auschwitz, their-- their clothes would be put in bags and hung up with numbers and so on. Every time they would be transferred-- to Buchenwald or whatever-- the bags had to be shipped out. And if they died, the bags were shipped to the families in Poland or wherever. Well, apparently, there was something going on there politically. It-- this particular unit was run by Polish-- Christian Polish prisoners-- women, political prisoners. Apparently there was some connection with the Polish underground. Whatever it was, they took away the women who were suspected, and they decided to substitute it with these Jewish women picked from the camp. Well, we were the lucky ones. Because this was now indoor labor, and it wasn't really labor. I learned how to pack there, because we had to pack and repack. All I know is that we still were sent back to camp to stay overnight and then back. And after awhile, they decided that they didn't want us going back to camp. So we really stayed in that unit. And the food was a little better-- wasn't much, but it was-- whatever it was, was twice a day, again, but it-- we could keep a little cleaner because we didn't sleep nine to a bunk. I don't know, maybe we sleep-- slept three to a bunk. I really don't remember-- but certainly not nine. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] having now worked in the marshes-- and this is the fall-- you see, the fall season. By now, it's about October, maybe November. Having gotten out of those marshes, you can imagine what stroke of luck this was to find ourselves under a roof, not standing in water, not being beaten on the marches. Because the march itself, going from the Auschwitz barrack to that-- to the marshes, that also took its toll because people were being shot on the way and coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't have to march to the work-- to the job, because the job was there. We slept indoors. We worked indoors. And I think this is really, in the final analysis, this phase would have finished us off-- although a lot of women from our hometown did survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because after-- you see, we were in that [GERMAN] unit until they were liquidating Auschwitz, which was about two months. And a lot of women who did not go with us and did join us on that death march later survived-- as I am here to attest, because I see some of them once a year when we meet for the [INAUDIBLE]. And of course, everybody knows what happened when the death march occurred.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, Auschwitz was evacuated on the 18th-- officially on the 18th of January 1945. Whether we were actually on the death march on the 19th or 17th or 16th or 18th, I could not tell you. But I would place it as the first half of January 1945. And we were told to just line up again and with-- got into a larger unit of women. I went always with women, you know-- never, never with men. And we-- this is January. It's bitter winter. Snow is falling. We're lining up to go we don't know where. And this was also another part-- you know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the fear-- you see, that fear that has somehow subsided. Not-- you know, we were now indoors, working indoors. And we thought, oh gee, maybe we will survive the war after all. You know. So it isn't that we weren't fearful, but we-- we-- we managed to live with the little fear that was left-- not little, but that fear that was already manageable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was a brand new experience with it-- absolutely no idea what this was about. While we were in Auschwitz, we already saw that they were sort of liquidating it, because while we were there, there was still one chimney smoking-- and the smell of it. You know? And the-- and the-- the fire, you could see it at night, and-- and we knew that people were being burned. And we knew that the-- the gypsies were killed off. And then we heard of the Hungarian transports going. We heard of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were trying to accommodate our orientation to what's happening-- that maybe something is really happening that's good, that-- that is maybe going to liberate us one way or another. Then we found ourselves on that road, on the death march, which was, of course, just the next step. Because as the Russian offensive was now coming closer-- as they sent us away from Starachowice camp when the Russian offensive started all the way out East-- now the Russians were much closer. So now they were evacuating these camps, meaning all these camps that were in western Poland. And Auschwitz was, you know, in western Poland. And on--
